question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: timeStampInNanos(Timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a submitted transaction with a Timestamp whose getSeconds() value is near Long.MAX_VALUE / NANOS_PER_SECOND so that Math.multiplyExact() throws ArithmeticException, causing the transaction ingestion pipeline to abort processing and drop the transaction entirely, resulting in denial of service for legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: timeStampInNanos(Timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a crafted Timestamp where getSeconds() is negative and getNanos() is a large positive value, causing Math.addExact() to overflow and throw ArithmeticException, bypassing timestamp ordering checks and allowing the transaction to be replayed or reordered in the ledger?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: convertToNanosMax(long, long)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with an extremely large positive seconds value so that convertToNanosMax() silently returns Long.MAX_VALUE instead of throwing, causing the transaction to be stored with a far-future timestamp that corrupts chronological ordering of the ledger state?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: convertToNanosMax(long, long)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a large negative seconds value so that convertToNanosMax() silently returns Long.MIN_VALUE, causing the transaction to be stored with a far-past timestamp that makes it appear to predate all existing transactions, corrupting historical ordering and potentially bypassing replay-protection checks?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: convertToNanos(long, long)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction timestamp where second=Long.MAX_VALUE and nanos=1 to trigger an ArithmeticException in Math.multiplyExact(), causing an unhandled exception that propagates up the call stack and crashes the transaction processing thread, resulting in a denial-of-service condition?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: timestampInNanosMax(Timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a null Timestamp field so that timestampInNanosMax() returns null, and if the caller does not null-check the return value, cause a NullPointerException that disrupts transaction processing and allows the attacker to selectively crash ingestion of specific transaction types?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: fromEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction referencing an EVM address whose first 12 bytes are all zeros (matching MIRROR_PREFIX) but whose last 8 bytes encode a system entity number <= 999, causing fromEvmAddress() to resolve the address to a system entity EntityId and allowing the attacker to tamper with transactions targeting system-reserved accounts?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: fromEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction with an EVM address that has all-zero first 12 bytes and a last 8 bytes encoding of 0, causing fromEvmAddress() to call EntityId.of() with num=0 and throw InvalidEntityException that is silently caught and returns null, leading to the transaction being processed with an unresolved entity reference that bypasses entity validation?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: isLongZeroAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a crafted 20-byte EVM address where bytes 0-11 are all zeros, causing isLongZeroAddress() to return true and fromEvmAddress() to interpret the address as a long-zero address, allowing the attacker to make a non-canonical EVM address collide with a legitimate Hedera entity ID and redirect transaction effects to a different account?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: fromTrimmedEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a short EVM address (fewer than 20 bytes) that, after zero-padding by fromTrimmedEvmAddress(), produces a long-zero address matching a legitimate system entity, causing the transaction to be attributed to the wrong entity and corrupting account state?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: fromTrimmedEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a 21-byte EVM address so that fromTrimmedEvmAddress() returns null due to the length check (evmAddress.length > EVM_ADDRESS_LENGTH), causing the caller to receive null and potentially skip entity resolution, allowing the transaction to proceed with an unvalidated target address?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: toEvmAddress(ContractID)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a ContractID whose ContractCase is EVM_ADDRESS containing a crafted ByteString that, when passed to toBytes(), returns a shared internal byte array via UnsafeByteOutput, and if the caller later mutates this array, cause the EVM address stored in the transaction record to be silently altered, tampering with the on-chain contract reference?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: toEvmAddress(ContractID)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a ContractID set to the default instance (ContractID.getDefaultInstance()), causing toEvmAddress() to throw InvalidEntityException, and if this exception is not properly handled upstream, crash the transaction processor and deny service to other users submitting valid transactions concurrently?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: toEvmAddress(long)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction referencing entity number Long.MAX_VALUE, causing toEvmAddress() to produce a 20-byte address via Bytes.concat(MIRROR_PREFIX, Longs.toByteArray(Long.MAX_VALUE)) that collides with or is indistinguishable from a crafted non-long-zero EVM address, enabling entity impersonation in transaction routing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getPublicKey(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction containing a malformed protobuf-encoded Key byte array that causes Key.parseFrom() to throw an exception, which is caught and logged but returns null, causing the transaction's key validation to silently pass with a null public key and allowing the transaction to be accepted without proper signature verification?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getPublicKey(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a deeply nested KeyList (e.g., KeyList containing a KeyList containing a KeyList) so that getPublicKey() returns null at depth > 2, causing the mirror node to store a null public key for the account, which could be exploited to bypass key-based authorization checks in subsequent transaction lookups?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getPublicKey(Key, int)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction with a ThresholdKey containing a single-element KeyList that itself contains a ThresholdKey, causing getPublicKey() to recurse to depth=3 and return null, bypassing the key extraction logic and causing the mirror node to record no public key for the signing entity, enabling future transaction forgery against that entity?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getPublicKey(KeyList, int)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a KeyList containing exactly 2 or more keys so that getPublicKey(KeyList, int) returns null, causing the mirror node to store no public key for a multi-sig account, which could allow an attacker to later submit transactions against that account without the mirror node being able to verify the signing key?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: sanitize(String)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a memo field containing only null characters (0x0000), causing sanitize() to replace them all with the Unicode replacement character (0xFFFD), and if the sanitized memo is used in downstream transaction matching or deduplication logic, cause a collision with a legitimate transaction that originally contained 0xFFFD characters, enabling transaction confusion attacks?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: sanitize(String)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a memo field containing a mix of null characters and valid Unicode, causing sanitize() to produce a string that matches the sanitized form of a different transaction's memo, enabling hash or index collisions in the mirror node's transaction deduplication or lookup tables?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: sanitize(String)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a memo containing other PostgreSQL-unsafe characters beyond null (e.g., lone surrogates, overlong UTF-8 sequences) that sanitize() does not handle, causing a database insertion error that drops the transaction from the mirror node's record, creating a gap in the transaction history that could be exploited to hide fraudulent activity?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: toBytes(ByteString)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction containing a LiteralByteString payload larger than UnsafeByteOutput.SIZE (16 bytes) so that toBytes() returns the raw internal byte array without copying, and if any downstream code mutates this array, silently alter the transaction's stored byte data, causing the mirror node to record a tampered version of the transaction?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: toBytes(ByteString)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction whose ByteString payload is exactly UnsafeByteOutput.SIZE (16 bytes) or smaller, causing toBytes() to fall through to byteString.toByteArray() and return a copy, while a slightly larger payload returns the shared internal array, creating an inconsistency in mutation safety that an attacker can exploit by timing their payload size to get shared-array semantics?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: fromBytes(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction where a byte array is wrapped via UnsafeByteOperations.unsafeWrap() in fromBytes(), and if the original byte array is later modified by another thread processing a concurrent transaction, cause the ByteString to reflect the modified bytes, resulting in a race condition that corrupts the transaction's stored data?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DomainUtils.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: trim(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a contract storage slot key consisting entirely of zero bytes, causing trim() to return ArrayUtils.EMPTY_BYTE_ARRAY, and if this empty key is used as a storage slot identifier, collide with other zero-trimmed keys from different transactions, causing cross-transaction storage slot confusion and state corruption?
